articles.title: SALTO TALARIS Total Ankle Arthroplasty at a Minimum of 10 Years of Follow-up: Survival and Radiographic and Clinical Outcomes in a Series of 144 Ankles.
articles.authors: Sadoun M, Gaudot F, Bauer T, Stiglitz Y
articles.population: Patients with end-stage ankle osteoarthritis treated with SALTO TALARIS total ankle arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Implant survival rates, range of motion, AOFAS clinical scores, and proportion of patients with forgotten joint sensation
articles.take_home: The SALTO TALARIS ankle replacement had a 94% survival rate at 10 years with a low reoperation rate and good clinical outcomes. Most patients reported good ankle function with limited pain, and over 40% reported their ankle felt like a normal joint.

articles.title: Can artificial intelligence provide reliable patient education in minimally invasive bunion surgery? A comparative study of ChatGPT 5.2 and Gemini.
articles.authors: Yuksel B, Ucar SB, Ozdemir E, Ayik G, Kaymakoglu M, Huri G
articles.sample_size: Ten frequently asked questions submitted to each AI model
articles.outcomes: Quality assessed by DISCERN and Likert scales; readability and actionability evaluated by PEMAT and Flesch-Kincaid Reading Ease
articles.take_home: ChatGPT and Gemini both provide reliable, high-quality information about minimally invasive bunion surgery, but neither gave practical guidance that patients could act on, and both used language that was more complex than ideal for general patients.

articles.title: Radiographic correction of medial-column malalignment after minimally invasive versus open talonavicular arthrodesis: A single-centre comparative cohort study.
articles.authors: Werneburg F, Beschauner J, Felsberg M, Rump C, Arbab D, Zeh A, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing talonavicular arthrodesis for medial-column malalignment
articles.outcomes: Radiographic medial-column alignment (Meary angle) and bone union rates
articles.take_home: Both minimally invasive and open surgery achieved similarly good straightening of the foot and comparable healing rates. The minimally invasive approach worked as well as traditional open surgery despite patients in the open group starting with more severe deformity.

articles.title: CT-confirmed union rates following ankle and hindfoot arthrodesis with and without biological augmentation: A retrospective series.
articles.authors: Matheron G, Pearse R, Dellis S, Lewis TL, Welck M, Patel S, et al.
articles.population: Adult patients undergoing ankle and hindfoot arthrodesis
articles.outcomes: CT-verified bone union at 12 weeks (defined as >= 50% osseous bridging); factors associated with nonunion
articles.take_home: Bone union rates after ankle and hindfoot fusion were high at over 92%, and were similar whether or not surgeons used bone graft to help healing. Previous fusion surgery nearby was the main factor linked to healing problems.
